votes.reason: The Python code correctly checks if n is greater than or equal to 8 and if it is even, matching the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly checks if n is greater than or equal to 8 and if n is even, which matches the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly checks if n is greater than or equal to 8 and if it is even, matching the Coq specification.

The Coq spec defines a Prop that n >= 8 and n is even (Nat.even n = true). The Python code returns True iff n >= 8 and n % 2 == 0, which is the same condition for non-negative integers. For concrete natural number inputs, both produce the same judgment about whether the condition holds.

The Coq spec defines a Prop that n >= 8 and n is even (Nat.even n = true). The Python code returns True iff n >= 8 and n % 2 == 0, which is the same condition for non-negative integers. For concrete natural number inputs, both produce the same boolean judgment.

The Coq spec defines a Prop that n >= 8 and n is even. The Python function returns True iff n >= 8 and n % 2 == 0 (i.e., n is even). For all concrete non-negative integer inputs, both produce the same judgment about whether n satisfies being at least 8 and even.
For natural-number inputs, both require n >= 8 and even; Python’s n % 2 == 0 matches Coq’s Nat.even n = true.
Both require n >= 8 and n even; for natural numbers this matches exactly.
Both require n >= 8 and n even; for natural-number inputs they agree, and Python’s behavior on negatives doesn’t contradict the Coq spec’s domain.
